steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence already has one main verb: ‘analyzed.’ The blank sits between ‘a team of researchers’ and the long phrase ‘to find connections between... and... geotagged social media posts.’ That phrase is a modifier explaining the team’s purpose — the blank must introduce a modifier, not a second main verb.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "were hoping": a finite past-tense verb. This creates two main verbs in the sentence (‘were hoping’ and ‘analyzed’) with no conjunction joining them — a run-on. ✗
  • "hoped": another finite past-tense verb. Same problem as A — two main verbs, no conjunction. ✗
  • "was hoping": a finite past progressive verb. Still a second main verb. The singular ‘was’ also disagrees with the plural ‘team of researchers’ in context. ✗
  • "hoping": a present participle. It creates a participial phrase (‘hoping to find connections...’) that modifies ‘a team of researchers’ without adding a second main verb. The sentence has exactly one main verb: ‘analyzed.’ ✓
That matches the correct answer — D is correct.
